headline: Companies Are Ditching Experiments & Going All-In on AI—But Most Still Can't Nail the Execution

stories.title: JPMorgan Chase Reclassifies AI From R&D to 'Core Infrastructure' Spending
stories.summary: JPMorgan just made a massive strategic move, reframing its AI investments as foundational infrastructure rather than experimental projects. The bank's doubling down on internal productivity, cybersecurity hardening, and retail personalization.
stories.takeaway: This shift signals that AI is transitioning from 'nice-to-have' projects to the literal backbone of enterprise operations—and JPM isn't waiting around.
stories.strategyTake: Moving AI from experimental R&D to core operational capability signals permanent business model restructuring, not temporary optimization.

stories.title: Block Cuts 4,000 Jobs (40% of Workforce) Explicitly Blaming AI Automation
stories.summary: Block (Square/Cash App's parent) just pulled back the curtain and told the truth: they're replacing humans with AI systems at scale. This is the biggest single AI-driven layoff in corporate history, and CEO Jack Dorsey was brutally candid about it.
stories.takeaway: This marks a watershed moment—companies are finally admitting publicly that AI isn't just optimizing work, it's straight-up replacing roles. The displacement is happening faster than reskilling timelines.
stories.strategyTake: Companies are moving from 'AI augments workers' narrative to actively replacing human roles with autonomous systems—and being transparent about it.

stories.title: OpenAI Plans to Nearly Double Workforce to 8,000 by End of 2026
stories.summary: While other tech companies are laying off, OpenAI is aggressively hiring—nearly doubling from 4,500 to 8,000 employees. They're betting big on scaling commercial products and enterprise solutions before the competition catches up.
stories.takeaway: OpenAI's expansion spree signals confidence in monetization pathways but also reveals the immense operational challenges of scaling frontier AI research and deployment simultaneously.
stories.strategyTake: Aggressive headcount expansion reflects confidence in near-term revenue inflection points and long-term competitive positioning—but cultural integration at scale is a massive execution risk.

stories.title: McKinsey: 88% of Orgs Use AI, But Only 6% Are Actually Winning (And Making Real Profit)
stories.summary: McKinsey's new data is lowkey brutal: nearly everyone's deploying AI, but most are still stuck in pilot hell. Only 6% of companies qualify as 'high performers' where AI drives 5%+ EBIT impact—the rest are seeing marginal efficiency gains at best.
stories.takeaway: The gap between AI adoption and actual value creation is widening. It's not a tech problem anymore—it's an org design & execution problem. Companies need to reimagine workflows, not just automate existing ones.
stories.strategyTake: The 'AI adoption' metric is becoming meaningless. Real competitive advantage goes to companies redesigning workflows end-to-end, not those automating broken processes.

watchNext.title: The 'Last Mile' Problem: Why Most AI Transformations Are Stalling Out
watchNext.why: Harvard Business Review just published an eye-opening breakdown of why companies with hundreds of pilots still can't scale—and it's not the models or the data. It's the gap between technical implementation and actual organizational change.

watchNext.title: AI Doesn't Reduce Work—It Intensifies It
watchNext.why: New research from UC Berkeley shows the dirty secret nobody wants to admit: AI tools don't actually lighten workloads. They create more complexity, higher expectations, and burnout. Essential read if you care about the human side of AI adoption.
